channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Xi Doubles Down on His City of the Future
item.description: The Chinese president faces an uphill battle in his hopes for a second capital.

item.title: Can Frederiksen Win Over Denmark—Again?
item.description: The incumbent prime minister hopes her handling of U.S. President Donald Trump’s Greenland threats will secure her a third term in snap elections.

item.title: Empty Words Don’t Open Straits
item.description: The gap between narrative and reality is only growing in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Autonomous swarms are the future of drone warfare
item.description: Much of the innovation is being carried out in Ukraine

item.title: Four Things the Gulf States Will Expect From the U.S. After the Iran War
item.description: Countries that host U.S. forces want to be partners, not just platforms.

item.title: Blackouts, Food Shortages, and Water Scarcity
item.description: With no oil shipments, Cuba’s humanitarian crisis has become dire.

item.title: Markets are gripped by an alarming cognitive dissonance
item.description: Investors all seem to think everyone else is wrong

item.title: China’s government both drives and constrains the rise of AI
item.description: Officials want to lead the world in the new technology but are still wary of disruption

item.title: The Iran War Is Coming for Your Grocery Bill
item.description: Prices won’t just be higher at the pump.

item.title: What a battle to reopen the Strait of Hormuz would look like
item.description: A lot of ships, aircraft and soldiers would have to spend a long time in harm’s way for uncertain results

item.title: The Economic Consequences of the Iran War Reverberate in South Korea
item.description: It’s not just energy: South Korea’s stock market, industrial supply chains, and export markets have all been impacted.

item.title: America’s War, America’s Recession
item.description: The precise magnitude of the shock the US economy will face as a result of its war of choice in Iran is difficult to predict, given the array of factors at play. But the US is poorly equipped to weather the likely effects on inflation and interest rates, and financial-sector fragility is a key reason why.

item.title: As an AI Scholar, I Am Now Putting a High Probability on an AI Doomsday
item.description: What happens when the house of cards collapses?

item.title: This Energy Shock Demands a Green Industrial Strategy
item.description: In an era of geopolitical tumult, economic resilience requires changing not only the kinds of energy we consume, but also how, where, and by whom things are produced. Fortunately, through mission-oriented green industrial strategies, governments can help secure living standards and build economic resilience simultaneously.

item.title: India, China, and How Not to Save the Brahmaputra
item.description: Instead of pursuing a diplomatic solution to manage shared waters, India is following a dam-for-dam policy.

item.title: Fraught Financing Will Further Weaken the Defense Industrial Base
item.description: The Pentagon’s new $200 billion private equity fund would harm the critical industries it aims to support.

item.title: Botswana prepares to take an even bigger gamble on diamonds
item.description: The African country wants to own more of De Beers, the firm that helped it to escape poverty

item.title: China’s new masterplan for its tech economy in 2030 and beyond
item.description: The Communist Party’s technological ambition is breathtaking

item.title: The World After Trump
item.description: Will there be order after chaos?

item.title: Kim Jong Un Formalizes South Korea as ‘Primary Hostile State’
item.description: Addressing the 15th SPA, Kim enshrined permanent enmity with Seoul and declared his regime “ready for any choice” from the United States.

item.title: US Institutional Decay Is Threatening Global Finance
item.description: Even through tumultuous political cycles, the Federal Reserve, the Securities and Exchange Commission, and the Federal Trade Commission have been able to signal that US markets operate under clear, impersonal, reliably enforced rules. But this is no longer true, and the implications for the rest of the world are dire.

item.title: What Iran Wants From the War
item.description: Coercive bargaining returns to the Persian Gulf.

item.title: Uzbekistan Takes Next Step on Nuclear Power Plant Journey
item.description: Concrete is being poured at a site in Jizzakh region which will host Uzbekistan’s first nuclear power plant.

item.title: China’s Afghanistan-Pakistan Mediation Efforts Cast Doubt on Its Influence in Both Countries
item.description: Beijing has proved unable to end or even shape the conflict – especially in Pakistan.

item.title: North Korea’s Risky Bet on Military AI
item.description: Integrating AI into the North Korean military comes with risks, from technological vulnerabilities to the potential for an inadvertent nuclear war.

item.title: Can Central Asia Become a New Hub in the Global Fertilizer Market?
item.description: Sanctions, logistical disruptions, and new conflicts — from the Black Sea to the Persian Gulf — have turned the fertilizer market into a key arena of geoeconomic competition.

item.title: Femicide Without Borders: Uzbek Women Abroad Still at Risk
item.description: The three horrific murders of Uzbek women in Turkiye shed harsh light on the risks female migrant workers face, usually at the hands of those closest to them.

item.title: A Golden Opportunity for a Beleaguered WTO
item.description: By adopting the Investment Facilitation for Development Agreement, the World Trade Organization would brighten developing economies’ future prospects at a time of rising economic uncertainty. This would also send a powerful signal that, despite the challenges it faces, the WTO can still deliver tangible results.

item.title: Lessons for Singapore from Trump’s War in Iran
item.description: A strategic location coupled with a close U.S. partnership makes for an attractive military target.

item.title: The Far Right’s False Pacifism Is Endangering Europe
item.description: Europe's nationalist parties talk endlessly about defending European countries, but they are the least willing to confront hostile foreign governments. By taking a weak security posture, these leaders are practically inviting other countries to escalate their threats to Europe’s way of life.

item.title: Iran’s Minorities Will Pay the Price for Trump’s War
item.description: A battered Iranian regime is looking for scapegoats.

item.title: Amending the Japan’s Constitution After the LDP’s Landslide Victory
item.description: The ruling coalition still faces serious hurdles.

item.title: How Iran’s Deterrence Collapsed Ahead of the War
item.description: A key failure in 2024 paved the way for U.S. and Israeli attacks.

item.title: Why China Is Stoking Division in Myanmar’s Anti-Junta Alliance
item.description: Beijing fears a failed state on its border.

item.title: Australia, Singapore Agree to Keep Oil and Gas Flowing Amid Global Supply Crisis
item.description: The arrangement reflects both the two nations' robust bilateral ties and their largely complementary energy needs.

item.title: Give Me Liberty and Give Me Taxes
item.description: Why democracies love taxation—and autocracies hate it.

item.title: China Is Squeezing Southeast Asia
item.description: As imbalances grow, a backlash is brewing.

item.title: Will Iran Turn to Terrorism?
item.description: A desperate regime might go after soft targets.

item.title: Myanmar, War, and Federalism: A Conversation With Joe Lo Bianco
item.description: Can state-based administrations forge a federalism that will keep the country together?

item.title: Vietnam, Russia Sign Agreement on Nuclear Power Plant Construction
item.description: The agreement was one of several energy-related deals that were signed on the first day of Prime Minister Pham Minh Chinh's official visit to Moscow.

item.title: Donald Trump’s latest climbdown suggests he wants to end the war
item.description: The divide between Israel and America is widening
